story:
Daisy was certain her Grade Four teacher, Miss Schofield, was a princess. The elegant long skirts, the fine gold jewelry, and the beautiful face were convincing enough, and the way Miss Schofield talked down to almost everyone—even Daisy’s parents—seemed like royal training. Daisy perched at her old wooden desk and tried to listen to a lecture on fractions, but the Princess was only repeating things Daisy already knew.

Outside, a summer storm gathered and tugged Daisy’s attention toward the window. Raindrops smacked the glass and slithered down like little serpents, ambushing other drops and swallowing them whole before sliding on. The trees beyond the pane swayed and danced, their leaves whispering as the wind stroked them. Swallows, finches, and willie wagtails tucked themselves deep in a paper-bark tree, and Daisy imagined herself among them—small, feathered, warm, lulled by the sighing wind and the—

“Daisy! Are you in there? Earth to Daisy!” Miss Schofield’s fingers snapped.

Dragged back from her daydream, Daisy shrank under her teacher’s glare and the expectant eyes of sixteen classmates. “Were you listening?” Miss Schofield asked, eyebrows raised.

“No, Miss Schofield. I’m sorry,” Daisy admitted, her voice trembling.

With a theatrical sigh, Miss Schofield tapped the chalkboard. “What is half of a quarter?”

Relief flooded Daisy. “One-eighth, Miss Schofield.”

“Correct. Sit up straight. Drift off again, and you’ll spend lunchtime in the classroom.”

Daisy pulled her shoulders back and fixed her eyes on the lesson. A princess deserved proper respect.

When Miss Schofield handed out a worksheet, Daisy set to work. Around her, one boy muttered through his math and a girl tapped her pen in a steady rhythm. The sounds almost braided themselves into music, but Daisy wrestled her focus back onto the page and solved each problem carefully. She finished a little after some but stood proudly in the line beside Miss Schofield’s desk, waiting for her paper to be marked.

The wind bullied the trees now, clawing at leaves and twigs and threatening to snap limbs, yet the trees stayed rooted and steady. Daisy wished she could sink herself into the floor like that, strong and unshakable.

At the desk, Miss Schofield flicked through answers with a green pen that few others used; Daisy liked to imagine green was the color of her royal house. The heavy gold bangle on Miss Schofield’s wrist clanked against the wood—surely a crown, shrunk and disguised. Daisy smiled as every answer earned a tick, but Miss Schofield’s lovely face went storm-dark.

“How did you get these right when you weren’t listening? Did you copy from Gemma?”

“No!” Daisy squawked, flushing, suddenly a small bird again longing for the shelter of branches. Miss Schofield pointed her to the front and crooked a finger at Gemma like a sorceress casting a spell. Gemma drifted forward, and Miss Schofield skimmed her sheet. A couple of Gemma’s answers were wrong. Daisy’s logic—that she couldn’t have copied from someone who’d finished later and made mistakes—did not interest the Princess.

“Fine,” Miss Schofield conceded. “You did it. Now put your name on it before I make you do another worksheet at lunchtime.”

“Yes, Your Highness,” Daisy said, before she could catch the words. Giggles rippled down the line.

Miss Schofield’s eyebrows soared; her fair face flushed almost purple. Her voice, calm and dangerous, pronounced, “For that, Daisy, you’ve earned yourself a lunchtime detention. I’ll be speaking with your parents. Back to your desk. I don’t want to see or hear you for fifteen minutes.”

Eyes down, trembling, Daisy obeyed. Outside, the storm raged; inside, she counted herself lucky not to be turned into a toad, and tried—like the trees—to anchor herself, stand tall, and keep her royal theories quietly tucked beneath her feathers.
